places.name: Alesund

visits.text: Ålesund's city centre is a unique case in Europe: on the night of January 23rd, 1904, a fire razed 850 houses in 16 hours and left 10,000 people homeless. The entire town was rebuilt in brick and concrete between 1904 and 1907 in the then-fashionable Art Nouveau style - hence this rare architectural coherence. Stroll along Kongens gate and St Olav square to see the turrets, ornate façades, and floral motifs.

The Jugendstilsenteret is housed in the former Svaneapoteket pharmacy from 1907, whose original interior has been preserved. The museum traces the 1904 fire, the accelerated reconstruction, and the Art Nouveau influences from Germany - Kaiser Wilhelm II, a regular visitor to Sunnmøre, had sent four relief ships after the disaster.

Ålesund's harbour and its quays facing the Sunnmøre Alps give the quintessential view of the town from the water. Walk to the end of Molovegen to see the old wooden warehouses - some converted into cafés - and the red lighthouse at the harbour entrance.

Runde island hosts up to 500,000 seabirds on its cliffs - including around 100,000 pairs of Atlantic puffins that arrive in April to nest until late July. It's Norway's southernmost bird cliff and one of the country's most important. The marked trail from Goksøyra to Lundeura takes 40-50 minutes at a normal pace.

Runde Fyr lighthouse, on the western point, marks the starting point of the coastal trail along the cliffs. The best time to see puffins is in the evening around 8-9pm, when they return to their burrows after a day of fishing at sea.

activities.text: Climb the 418 steps to the Aksla viewpoint in Byparken park. The view over Ålesund and its islands, with the Sunnmøre Alps as backdrop, is the finest in town. The step numbers are printed on the risers if you need to keep count.

Excursion to Runde to observe puffins from May to July. The island is accessible by bridge from the mainland - allow 2 hours' drive from Ålesund. Stick to marked trails: bird protection has been in force since 1957 and leaving the paths during nesting season is prohibited.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim
Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim

map_keywords: Ålesund Art Nouveau city centre Norway

visits.text: The Hjemmeluft rock carvings are Norway's only UNESCO-listed prehistoric site (since 1985)—and the largest ensemble of hunter-gatherer rock art in Northern Europe. Over 6,000 carvings depicting elk, reindeer, boats, and hunting scenes were created between 4200 and 500 BC. Follow the 3km of wooden boardwalks winding among the boulders.

The Alta Museum, installed directly on the rock carving site since 1991, presents carving techniques, the life of Arctic hunter-gatherers, and the northern lights—the first permanent aurora observatory was built in Alta in the late 19th century.

Alta's Nordlyskatedralen (Northern Lights Cathedral), inaugurated in 2013, stands out for its titanium spiral rising toward the sky. It evokes a northern light frozen in architecture—the visit is brief but the exterior is worth the detour.

The Alta River is one of the world's most coveted salmon rivers—Atlantic salmon regularly exceed 25kg here, and fishing rights for non-residents reach the equivalent of 30,000 NOK for three days. Each year, a lottery distributes 70 fishing days to outside anglers among 1,200 applicants. Even without a fishing rod, walking along the banks under the midnight sun is breathtaking.

The Sami Siida, just outside Alta, is a traditional Sami camp where you can feed reindeer, listen to stories around the fire, and taste bidos—Sami reindeer stew—in a real lavvu. It's one of the most accessible Sami cultural experiences from Alta.

Kautokeino is the heart of Sami culture in Norway: 90% of its inhabitants are Sami and the Sami language is spoken daily. A 130km detour from the main road—but it's one of the rare municipalities where you'll truly feel the presence of a distinct culture, with its traditional dress, artisans, and church.

The return to Alta through Finnmark's interior unfolds landscapes of tundra, rivers, and dwarf birch forests—the great inhabited emptiness of the end of the world. In autumn, the ochre and golden colors of the tundra are spectacular.

activities.text: The Sautso canyon on the Alta River is Northern Europe's deepest canyon with walls reaching 400m height over 22km length. Boat excursions allow you to travel alongside the cliffs from Lake Storvatnet—check local departures from Alta.

In winter, go dog sledding on the Finnmarksvidda—Norway's largest plateau stretches endlessly without a light or tree in sight. Local mushers offer 2-3 hour outings with huskies. In summer, guided hikes with the dogs are also organized.

Alta is one of the world's northern lights capitals in winter—the surrounding countryside, far from city lights, offers a perfectly dark sky. The best nights are from September to March, between 6pm and 2am.

The Alta River is renowned for world-class salmon fishing—local guides organize outings on the water in summer, fly-fishing or spinning. In summer, you can also rent a canoe to paddle along the riverbanks at your own pace.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape
Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape
Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape

visits.text: Gudbrandsjuvet, along the national tourist route, is a narrow gorge with steel walkways suspended above waterfalls. Stop for 20 minutes - it's exemplary viewpoint architecture and the place empties quickly.

Trollstigen - literally 'the Troll Ladder' - climbs 11 hairpin bends at 9% gradient to 858 metres altitude, with the Stigfossen waterfall (320 m drop) right beside the road. Opened on July 31st, 1936 by King Haakon VII after 8 years of hand construction, each bend bears the name of the foreman who built it.

From the panoramic platform at the top of Trollstigen, designed by Reiulf Ramstad Architects, you look down on the hairpins below and the Isterdalen valley opening towards the Atlantic. Allow 30 minutes up there - the visitor centre café is worth the stop.

Åndalsnes, Norway's mountaineering capital, nestles at the head of Romsdalsfjord beneath Romsdalshorn (1,550 m). It's an ideal base for hikers - the Norsk Tindesenter on the harbour houses the mountaineering museum and Norway's tallest indoor climbing wall.

Trollveggen is Europe's highest vertical rock face - 1,100 metres of gneiss from the valley floor to the summit, with a 50-metre overhang at the top. You can see it from the E136, 10 km from Åndalsnes. The first ascents didn't happen until 1965 - a British amateur team, mocked by experts, had taken up the challenge.

Trollveggen visitor centre, at the foot of the wall on the E136, offers a panoramic terrace and a film on the history of climbing attempts and BASE jumping - banned since 1986 after several fatal accidents.

activities.text: Drive the Geiranger-Trollstigen tourist route (around 104 km). Trollstigen is closed from November to mid-May depending on snow conditions - check vegvesen.no before departure. Vehicles over 12.4 metres are prohibited.

Hike to Rampestreken from Åndalsnes - a cantilevered steel platform 550 metres above Romsdalsfjord. Allow 2h30 return over 4 km. The ascent is steep but the trail is marked. The view over the valley and fjords is worth every metre of elevation.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim
Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim

visits.text: On the E16 between Gudvangen and Voss, the road climbs Nærøydalen valley through a series of tight hairpin bends - 21 serpentine turns in a few kilometres. Walls drop sheer on either side. Take your time: viewpoints over the valley and waterfalls are plentiful and accessible directly from the road.

Upon arrival in Bergen, stroll the Hanseatic wharf of Bryggen, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1979. In 1360, the Hanseatic League established its trading office here and controlled the dried fish trade for nearly 400 years - Bergen is the only non-League city whose original medieval structure still stands within the urban fabric. The current appearance dates from the reconstruction after the 1702 fire; 62 buildings survive.

Explore the alleys and interior warehouses of Bryggen - the facade along the wharf is what everyone photographs, but the back of the rows of houses is far more interesting. You'll find craft galleries, jewellers' and potters' workshops set in spaces that haven't changed since the 18th century. In high season, arrive before 9am to have the alleys to yourself.

Fisketorget fish market is one of Norway's oldest open-air markets. You'll find fresh salmon, langoustines and halibut - plus prepared food stalls. The adjacent indoor market, open year-round, is the better option outside summer.

The Nordnes peninsula, at the western tip of Bergen's centre, offers a pleasant walk along the shore with views over Byfjord. Nordnesparken at the point is where Bergen locals come to swim in summer - the water is cold but the setting is beautiful.

Fantoft Stave Church, in Paradis south of Bergen, is a reconstruction. The original, a 12th-century stavkirke moved from Fortun in 1883, burned in arson in 1992. The identical reconstruction was completed in 1997. Visit the exterior and grounds - the carved doors with intertwined dragon motifs are particularly detailed.

The Milde Arboretum, in southern Bergen, is one of Norway's largest botanical gardens with 50 hectares of rhododendron, conifer and coastal plant collections. Trails wind through coves and mossy woods - this is quiet, non-touristy Bergen, 15 km from the centre.

activities.text: Drive from Flåm to Bergen via E16 and RV13: allow 3 hours, including photo stops in Nærøydalen valley. The section between Gudvangen and Voss is the most spectacular - don't rush through it.

Take the Fløibanen funicular from the city centre up to Mount Fløyen (320 m) - in service since 1918, 850 metres of track, 6 minutes uphill. The panoramic view over Bergen, its seven mountains and Byfjord is the city's essential postcard shot. At the summit, marked trails lead into the troll forest - allow 30 to 45 minutes for the loop around Lake Skomakerdiket. Book online to skip the queue in summer.

Hike through the coastal woods and rocky coves of Milde - the trails around the arboretum extend several kilometres toward the fjord. The terrain is easy, ideal for a morning walk in Norwegian nature without leaving Bergen.

days_plan.itin_title: Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen

visits.text: The ferry crossing from Svolvær (or Skutvik) to Bodø across the Vestfjorden lasts between 3h30 and 4h30 depending on departure. Settle on the upper deck if weather permits: the view of the Lofoten receding to the north is spectacular. Bodø lies 33 km north of the Arctic Circle.

Bodø's waterfront was entirely rebuilt after the World War II bombings (May 1940) that destroyed 75% of the city. Today, the harbor faces the offshore islands and the mountains of Salten. It's an active city, departure point for ferries to the Lofoten and to Svalbard.

The Norwegian Aviation Museum (Norsk Luftfartsmuseum) opened in 1994—the building itself, seen from the air, traces the shape of an airplane propeller over 10,000 m². Inside, you'll encounter civilian and military aircraft from World War II to the present day, including a replica of the American U2 spy plane shot down in 1960 as it headed toward Bodø from Pakistan. Don't miss the flight simulator.

activities.text: The Saltstraumen, 33 km from Bodø, is the world's strongest tidal current: every 6 hours, 400 million cubic meters of water squeeze through a 150-meter-wide strait at speeds up to 20 knots (37 km/h), creating whirlpools 10 meters in diameter. Check schedules online before heading out—the phenomenon is invisible at slack tide. The effect is strongest on days of full and new moon.

days_plan.itin_title: Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: The Jæren Scenic Route follows 70 km of flat, windswept coastline - a geographical curiosity in Norway, land of mountains. Wild sand beaches, dunes, and lighthouses unfold along this route protected since 1977.

Obrestad lighthouse (Obrestad fyr), built in solid granite in 1873, is the first land-based lighthouse on the Jæren coast. In its basement, German soldiers left murals during the occupation - accessible to visitors.
Egersund's historic town center lines up its painted wooden houses along the harbor - preserved maritime atmosphere and a few waterfront cafes. Allow 45 minutes to explore at leisure.

activities.text: The beaches of Bore and Orrestranden are among Norway's finest: Orrestranden stretches for 3 km of white sand, Borestranden is the Jæren's premier surf spot. Wind, waves, and an open horizon on the North Sea - a complete change of scenery.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast

visits.text: Leaving Oslo on the E16, stop at Sundvollen for views over Tyrifjorden - one of Norway's largest lakes, nestled between conifer forests and hills. The road then follows the Hallingdal valley on the RV7, with its red wooden farmhouses scattered across the pastures.

On the E16 between Lærdal and Aurland, you enter the Lærdal Tunnel - 24.5 km long, making it the world's longest road tunnel, opened in 2000. To break the monotony, engineers carved out three large caverns lit in blue and yellow at regular intervals. Don't speed: the limit is 80 km/h.

Before entering the tunnel, stop on the scenic Aurlandsfjellet road (RV243) if conditions allow: the plunging views over Aurlandsfjord from 1,300 metres elevation are spectacular. Warning - this road closes in winter.

The Flåmsbana Museum, free admission at the foot of the station, retraces the 20 years of construction of the Flåm Railway (1923-1940). The 20 km line descends 866 metres between Myrdal and Flåm, with a maximum gradient of 5.5% - one of the steepest in the world for standard gauge. 18 of the 20 tunnels were carved by hand by teams of 120 to 220 workers.

Otternes Bygdetun is a hamlet of historic farms perched on a promontory above Aurlandsfjord, at 300 metres elevation. Access on foot from Flåm in 45 minutes uphill (marked trail). The wooden houses, inhabited until the 1980s, have kept their original appearance. The elevated view of the fjord from the hamlet meadows is worth the effort.

Aurlandsvangen, the main town of Aurland municipality 7 km from Flåm, has a wooden church from the 1600s and a quiet waterfront on Aurlandsfjord. Stop by late afternoon to watch the evening light on the calm fjord waters.

From the electric boat, the walls of Nærøyfjord rise 1,800 metres - and the fjord is only 250 metres wide at its narrowest. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2005, it has remained untouched because no road was ever built along its shores: you'll pass farms accessible only by boat. Book tickets ahead in summer - boats leave full.

Gudvangen, at the terminus of Nærøyfjord, is a village of a few dozen inhabitants squeezed between vertiginous cliffs. Its name means 'field of the gods at the water's edge' in Old Norse. Stop to see Kjelfossen waterfall, which plunges more than 800 metres from the heights.

activities.text: Driving route Oslo-Flåm via E16: allow 4 to 5 hours total, including breaks. The road is passable year-round but winter conditions may apply from November to April. Check vegvesen.no for road conditions before departure.

Hike or photo walk along the Flåm harbour and the shore of Aurlandsfjord. The village is tiny - a few hundred inhabitants - but the setting between fjord and towering mountains is arresting. In high season, dozens of cruise ships anchor in the bay: arrive early morning for peace and quiet.

Silent cruise by electric boat on Nærøyfjord between Flåm and Gudvangen - 2 hours one way. The boat 'Future of the Fjords', fully electric since 2018, glides soundlessly between the fjord walls. The absence of combustion engine is striking: you hear only waterfalls and eagle cries. Several departures daily, year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen

visits.text: Flydalsjuvet viewpoint, accessible by car from Geiranger, gives the postcard view with the fjord below and tiny Geiranger village at the back. This is where most aerial photos of the fjord are taken - go early morning to avoid the coaches.

Ørnesvingen (the Eagle's Bend), on the tourist route descending towards Geiranger, offers a direct panorama of the Seven Sisters and the fjord from the clifftop. On clear days, you can see the green water 600 metres below.

The Norwegian Fjord Centre (Norsk Fjordsenter) traces the history of inhabitants who lived on the farms overlooking Geirangerfjord. The fjord has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2005 - both for its landscapes and for the traces of agricultural life in extreme conditions.

Dalsnibba (1,476 m) is Europe's highest car-accessible viewpoint above a fjord. The toll road climbs in hairpins from Geiranger - from the top, the cruise ships below look like toys. The Skywalk platform cantilevers out over the void above the fjord.

activities.text: Hellesylt-Geiranger cruise through the UNESCO Geirangerfjord. The Seven Sisters (De sju systre) cascade 410 metres in seven parallel streams down the fjord's north wall - opposite, the Suitor (Friaren) answers them from the other shore according to local legend. The journey takes around 1h30 depending on conditions.

Hike to the abandoned Skageflå farm, perched 250 metres above the fjord. Take the cruise out from Geiranger to be dropped at the foot of the cliff, then climb the steep trail to the empty farm buildings. The view of the Seven Sisters from the same level is unprecedented.

Kayak on Geirangerfjord at the foot of the waterfalls - the silence between the 1,700-metre walls is striking. Set out early morning when the cruise ships haven't yet arrived. Rental companies offer equipment and guides from Geiranger harbour.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim
Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim

visits.text: The Struve Meridian Column at Fuglenes (UNESCO since 2005) is the northernmost point of a network of 265 triangulation stations stretching from Norway to the Black Sea—a 19th-century scientific project to measure the exact shape of the Earth. The walk to the monument offers a fine view over the harbor.

The Gjenreisningsmuseet tells how retreating Nazi forces razed Hammerfest in 1944—only the cemetery chapel remained standing. Audio testimonies from survivors, photographs, and period interior reconstructions show what it meant to rebuild everything from scratch. Allow at least an hour.
Hammerfest Church, rebuilt in 1961, has a triangular form inspired by traditional Sami tents—a subtle echo of the region's Sami past in a resolutely modern building.

The Isbjørnklubben (Royal Polar Bear Club) is a local institution founded in 1963—you can become a member by signing the register, earning you a stamped diploma. A curiosity not to miss if you're passing through.

activities.text: Stroll along Hammerfest's waterfront: the harbor is lively, the multicolored 1950s houses tell the story of reconstruction, and you can watch seabirds from the pier in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape

visits.text: The E6 then E69 route crosses the Finnmark tundra—a treeless plateau where reindeer roam freely across the road. Plan to slow down: herds cross at all hours and they don't move aside quickly.

The North Cape Tunnel (Nordkapptunnelen) plunges 212m beneath the sea to reach the island of Magerøya—6.8km of undersea road tunnel with a toll at the exit. It's the gateway to Honningsvåg and North Cape.
Honningsvåg is often presented as the world's northernmost village connected by road. Its colorful harbor, some 2,800 inhabitants, and bright houses give a human scale to this edge of the continent.

The North Cape plateau extends 307m above the Arctic Ocean: this is where the metal globe erected in 1978 stands, symbol of continental Europe's northernmost point. On clear days, you gaze toward the North Pole with no land in between except Svalbard, 2,100km away.

The Nordkapphallen is largely carved into the rock: it houses a panoramic cinema showing North Cape through the four seasons, an ecumenical chapel (Sankt Johanneskapell), and a permanent exhibition. Count on an hour to see everything—admission is charged.

Make the detour to Gjesvær to reach the cliffs of Gjesværstappan, one of Norway's largest seabird colonies—puffins, gannets, and cormorants nest here en masse. Boat excursions depart from the village.

activities.text: The road from Honningsvåg to North Cape crosses the island of Magerøya: a 34km route between lakes, tundra, and sheep grazing freely along the roadside. In summer, not a tree blocks the horizon—the landscape is lunar and the sky immense.

From May 13 to July 29, the sun never sets at North Cape—it traces a complete circle around the horizon, bathing the cliff in permanent golden light. Stay until midnight to experience this spectacle from the cliff edge: few experiences compare.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape
Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape

visits.text: The ferry crossing from Lysebotn to Forsand ascends the entire Lysefjord from the bottom - you'll see the walls of Kjerag and Preikestolen along the fjord's axis. Allow about 1h30 of navigation depending on schedules. Check departures in advance, they're limited.
Stroll through Forsand village at the foot of Lysefjord - a few colorful houses, a quiet harbor, and views of the fjord walls from the quay. A good break after the previous days' efforts.

activities.text: Flørli houses the world's longest wooden staircase: 4,444 steps climbing 740 m of elevation along the old hydroelectric pipeline built between 1917 and 1921. The hamlet has no road access - you must take the ferry. At the top, Lake Ternevatnet and a plunging view over Lysefjord; descend via the Rallarstien to complete the loop.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast

visits.text: Posebyen, Kristiansand's historic quarter, survived the great fire of 1892 - the other neighborhoods burned, this one didn't. Founded in 1641 by King Christian IV, the city preserves its grid layout and single-story white wooden houses here. A peaceful atmosphere a stone's throw from the harbor.
Christiansholm fortress, completed in 1672 on an islet facing the harbor, was attacked only once in 1807, by the British navy. Its walls reach 5 m thick. Free entry, open April to October.
Fiskebrygga, Kristiansand's fish market, animates the waterfront with its boats and seafood stalls. A good vantage point for the harbor and the archipelago stretching before the city.
Ravnedalen is a nature park right inside Kristiansand: ponds, waterfalls, and a small open-air stage amid the rocks. A quiet stroll 10 minutes' walk from the center, beloved by locals.
Kristiansand Cathedral (Kristiansand domkirke), built in 1884 in neo-Gothic style, is Norway's third-largest church with 1,800 seats. You'll see its brick facade from the main square.

activities.text: Take a boat to explore Kristiansand's archipelago - small granite islands, colorful summer houses, and swimming in sheltered coves. In summer, regular services depart from the harbor for nearby islands.

Bragdøya island is accessible by local ferry from central Kristiansand - coastal trails, rocky beaches, and swimming in the calm archipelago waters. Perfect for closing out this road trip on a gentle note.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast
Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast

visits.text: Bud village, at the entrance to the Atlantic Road, was the most active trading centre between Bergen and Trondheim in the 16th-17th centuries. Today a few dozen red wooden houses face the open ocean - the contrast with the raging Hustadvika is striking in stormy weather.

The Atlantic Ocean Road (Atlanterhavsveien) links 8.3 km of islets via 8 bridges open to the Norwegian Sea - built between 1983 and 1989, after weathering 12 violent storms during construction. It was voted Norway's 'construction of the century' in 2005. In bad weather, waves literally wash over the bridges.

Storseisundet Bridge, 260 metres long, is the most photographed on the route. Its cantilevered curve creates an optical illusion from certain angles: the road appears to plunge straight into the ocean. Stop at the viewpoint lay-by for the photo - you can't get it from the car.

activities.text: Drive the Atlantic Road in rough weather if you can time it - waves washing over the bridges are part of the experience. Plan frequent stops at the four purpose-built viewpoints along the route.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim

visits.text: Driving along the Lyngenfjord on the E6, you'll see the Lyngen Alps rise before you—an alpine mountain range carrying over 140 glaciers, just a stone's throw from the Arctic Ocean. Stop at the viewpoints to photograph the glaciers reflecting in the fjord.
Lyngseidet is the main village on the Lyngen Peninsula: fjord on one side, glacier-clad walls on the other. This is where the ferry departs to cross the Lyngenfjord—count on a 20-minute crossing.

activities.text: The hike to Blåisvatnet (Blue Lake) is the most popular in the Lyngen Alps: 8km round trip, 189m elevation gain, relatively flat terrain except for the last rocky sections before the lake. Its intense turquoise color comes from meltwater from the Lenangsbreen glacier just above. Bring good shoes—the ground is rocky all the way.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape

visits.text: The road from Sirdal to Lysebotn crosses a mountain plateau where you might spot wild reindeer - keep your eyes on the roadside. The landscape changes dramatically as you descend toward the fjord.
Lysevegen: 27 dizzying switchbacks descend 640 m of elevation to reach the bottom of Lysefjord. Take your time on the bends - a few viewpoints let you grasp the scale of the descent.
Lysebotn has about ten permanent residents and serves as a base for BASE jumpers from around the world, drawn by Kjerag's walls. A place apart, at the end of the world, at the bottom of a fjord.

activities.text: Kjeragbolten is that 5 m³ boulder wedged between two cliffs 984 m above Lysefjord. The hike is 11 km round trip with 700 m elevation gain: metal chains on the steep sections help you climb, but the slabs can be slippery in wet weather. Allow 6 hours. Start early to avoid the queue at the boulder.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast

visits.text: Flekkefjord and its Dutch quarter (Hollenderbyen) line up white facades reminiscent of North Sea trading towns - this architecture reveals old commercial ties with the Netherlands. An easy stop en route.

Sjøsanden beach in Mandal, 800 m long, is regularly voted Norway's best beach. It stretches within the Furulunden nature park, a 10-minute walk from the center - fine sand, calm water, pine forest backdrop.
Mandal's old town unfolds its winding lanes and white wooden houses west of the harbor. Birthplace of sculptor Gustav Vigeland, whose works adorn Oslo's great park.

activities.text: Lindesnes lighthouse is Norway's first lighthouse - the first lantern was lit here in 1656, and the current cast-iron tower dates from 1915. You're standing at mainland Norway's southernmost point, 2,518 km from the North Cape. The coastal trail from the parking lot is 3 km round trip with some rocky passages.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast

visits.text: Nusfjord is one of Norway's oldest fishing villages, with traces of human settlement dating back to 425 BC. In 1975, it was designated one of three UNESCO pilot projects in Norway for the preservation of traditional wooden assemblages. Today home to just 25 permanent residents, the village nonetheless welcomes 80,000 visitors per year. An entrance fee is charged in summer to contribute to site conservation.

Ramberg beach offers white sand with turquoise waters backed by mountains—the kind of panorama you'd expect more in the tropics. In July, you can swim here. Don't expect solitude: this is one of the most frequented beaches in the Lofoten in summer.

The Lofotr Viking Museum at Borg is built around the largest Viking longhouse ever discovered in Europe, unearthed in 1983 by a farmer plowing his fields. The foundation measured 83 meters long by 9.5 meters wide, dated to around 500 AD. The 1995 reconstruction lets you walk through the interior, see replica Viking ships, and watch craft demonstrations.

activities.text: The coastal trail from Nusfjord to Nesland runs past rocky coves, free-roaming sheep, and views over the fjord. Allow 2h30 round-trip over 7 km with no difficult elevation gain. The trail is poorly marked toward the end—download a GPS track before setting out.

days_plan.itin_title: Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands

places.name: Oslo

visits.text: Follow the Akerselva from Grünerløkka down to the waterfalls and old mills - 9.8 km of trail threading through Oslo's industrial history. Back in 1856, the river's 20 cascades powered around twenty textile factories and flour mills. Walk it north to south: the view of the Opera House and fjord comes as a grand finale.

The waterfront at Aker Brygge and Tjuvholmen shows you two faces of Oslo along the Oslofjord: former shipyards converted into restaurants and galleries on one side, the contemporary jetty at Tjuvholmen on the other. Walk out to the end of the pier for views across the fjord islands.

Climb onto the marble roof of Oslo Opera House (Operahuset) - it's free, open all the time, and the view over the fjord is the best in the city. The building, designed by Snøhetta and opened in 2008, is clad in Carrara marble and white granite. It won the Mies van der Rohe Prize in 2009. Pack slip-resistant shoes in winter: the roof gets treacherous.

Take a turn through the tropical glasshouses at Oslo's Botanical Garden (Botanisk hage), in Tøyen. The garden has been around since 1814 and holds one of Europe's largest collections of Nordic plants. Free admission to the outdoor park.

The Barcode Project, in the Bjørvika district, lines up a series of towers with striped facades designed by different architecture firms between 2005 and 2016. View them from the waterfront promenade - the barcode effect reads more clearly from a distance.

Climb up to the Damstredet and Telthusbakken neighbourhood, a ten-minute walk from the centre: you'll find painted wooden houses from the 18th and 19th centuries that survived Oslo's successive rebuildings. A striking contrast with the glass towers down by the water.

Sagene, Oslo's former industrial heartland, is now one of the city's most sought-after neighbourhoods. The name comes from Old Norwegian 'sager', meaning sawmills - the first date back to the 16th century. Wander past red-brick facades and former warehouses now converted into workshops and cafés.

The Norsk Folkemuseum at Bygdøy gathers over 150 historic buildings relocated from across Norway - it's one of Europe's largest open-air museums. The centrepiece is the Gol Stave Church, built around 1200 in the Hallingdal valley. In 1880, the parish of Gol wanted to demolish it to build a new one. The Society for the Preservation of Ancient Monuments bought it, and King Oscar II funded its transfer to Bygdøy in 1884. On one of the nave pillars, a runic inscription reads: 'Kiss me, for I struggle.' Allow 2 hours to tour the whole museum.

The Viking Ship Museum (Vikingskipshuset) houses three longships from the 9th and 10th centuries, including the Oseberg, discovered in 1904 in a burial mound near Tønsberg. It's one of the best-preserved medieval wooden artefacts in the world - the find also included sledges, tapestries, and everyday objects. Note that the museum reopened after renovation in 2026 as the Museum of the Vikings.

The Holmenkollen Ski Museum is the world's oldest ski museum - it opened in 1923 and traces 4,000 years of skiing history, from Stone Age rock carvings to the gear from Fridtjof Nansen and Roald Amundsen's polar expeditions. The facade was completely renovated by Snøhetta in 2023. The building is carved into the mountain, beneath the ski jump.

From the viewing platform atop Holmenkollbakken - a ski jump built in 1892 and rebuilt for the 1952 Oslo Olympics - you get the panoramic view of the city and Oslofjord that every guidebook describes. It's deserved. Ride the elevator, then 114 steps to the top. Allow 30 to 40 minutes to explore the summit. Avoid competition days if you want the terrace to yourself.

On the RV7, the crossing of Hardangervidda gives you 67 km of lunar landscape - northern Europe's largest high-altitude plateau, at 1,100-1,200 metres. Lake after lake, open heath and infinite horizons. It's also one of the few places in Europe where wild reindeer migrate freely: several thousand animals cross the plateau with the seasons.

At Hol, on the RV7, stop to see Hol Gamle Kyrkje (the old church of Hol), a medieval stone church dating from the 12th century. A brief 15-20 minute stop suffices to view the exterior and historic cemetery - it's one of the best-preserved old stones in the region.

activities.text: Photo walk through Sentrum: the colourful facades and heritage buildings of the historic centre are particularly photogenic late in the day when raking light cuts through the narrow streets. Head from City Hall toward the Parliament for an hour of urban wandering.

Round-trip ferry ride from Aker Brygge to Bygdøy - about 10 minutes across the Oslofjord. The public ferry (line 91) runs May to September. It's the most pleasant way to reach the Bygdøy peninsula and its museums, with fine views of the fjord islands.

Holmenkollen hosts annual biathlon and ski jumping competitions on the national arena - dates vary according to the FIS and IBU calendars. Outside competition season, the cross-country ski trails of Nordmarka start just behind the jump. Reach Holmenkollen by metro (line 1, Holmenkollen station, 25 minutes from the centre).

Return drive Ulvik-Oslo via RV7 and E16: allow 4 to 5 hours total, including breaks. Pass through Geilo to refuel if needed - it's the only real stop on the plateau. Check conditions on vegvesen.no in autumn and spring: the RV7 can close during snowstorms on the plateau.

days_plan.itin_title: Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen

map_keywords: Gol Stave Church Norwegian Museum of Cultural History

visits.text: Reine is nestled between sharp peaks and the blue-green waters of the Reinefjorden—the most reproduced postcard image of the Lofoten. The light here is spectacular at any hour, but especially at dusk. Park at the harbor and explore the lanes around the rorbu on foot.

The Fredvang bridges span several islets between Flakstad and Moskenesøy with plunging views over the Selfjorden. Make a photo stop on the roadside: the perspective on the arched bridges with mountains behind is one of the most photographed in the Lofoten.

The hamlet of Hamnøy, 5 minutes from Reine, is home to the most photographed red rorbu in the Lofoten, facing the Reinefjorden and sheer rock walls. This is the most accessible sunset and midnight sun spot from Reine. Plan to be here early morning or late evening to avoid crowds of photographers.

Å i Lofoten is the last village at the end of the E10 highway, whose name is a single letter meaning "river" in Old Norse. This was where the end of the world stopped before modern tunnels. The Norwegian Fishing Village Museum (Norsk Fiskeværsmuseum), founded in 1988, brings together several period buildings including a cod liver oil factory still in working order and an 1878 bakery. In summer, fresh cinnamon rolls from the stone oven await you here.

The Stockfish Museum (Lofoten Tørrfiskmuseum), set in a former fish landing hall, explains the entire cod-drying process—from fishing in January to export to Italy. Lofoten stockfish is now a protected designation of origin at the European level, just like Roquefort cheese. If you've seen cod-drying racks all along the route, this is where you'll understand why.

activities.text: Reinebringen is the most famous hike in the Lofoten, with 1,978 stone steps built by Nepalese Sherpas between 2016 and 2021 to stabilize the trail. The summit reaches 448 meters, with a plunging view over Reine, Hamnøy, and the Reinefjorden. Allow 2h30 to 3h round-trip. Avoid in wet weather—the steps become slippery and visibility disappears. In July, start before 8 am to avoid queues on the trail.

The hike to the hidden beach at Kvalvika from Fredvang crosses moorland and low ridges with views over the Selfjord. The beach, accessible only on foot, remains wild and little frequented outside of July. Allow 3h round-trip over 6 km.

Sea kayaking among the islets of the Reinefjorden is the ideal evening activity—in summer, you paddle under the midnight sun between rock walls plunging straight into the water. Operators offer rentals and guided trips departing from Reine.

days_plan.itin_title: Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands
Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: The Senja National Scenic Route (Nasjonal turistveg Senja) connects Gryllefjord in the west to Botnhamn in the north. This is the route Norwegians themselves call the most beautiful in the national network. Take your time: every bend reveals cliffs 700 to 800 meters tall plunging straight into the Atlantic, hidden coves, and isolated villages.

Husøy is an active fishing village perched on its own islet in the Øyfjorden, connected to Senja by a 300-meter causeway. The colorful houses are anchored with cables to the ground to withstand storms. Local legend has it that the custom of the midday siesta, still practiced here, was introduced by Spanish Armada sailors shipwrecked on these shores in 1588.

The Bergsbotn viewpoint juts out over the Bergsfjorden and is easily reached from the road. It frames the fjord and ridges like a painting. Be wary of the narrow access road leading up—it's very tight with motorhomes coming the other way.

From Tungeneset beach, a Siberian larch boardwalk crosses the rocks toward a promontory facing the Okshornan—the Devil's Teeth, three granite peaks surging from the ocean. Pools on the rocks reflect the summits in calm weather: bring a tripod for your photos. It's also one of the island's best spots for northern lights at night.

Ersfjordstranda beach is a strip of white sand framed by steep mountains—the kind of landscape you don't expect at this latitude. Don't miss the famous golden cabin: the stainless steel gold-plated public toilet, which has become an attraction in its own right on the island.

activities.text: Ferry crossing from Tromsø to Senja via the Brensholmen–Botnhamn route—about a 45-minute link that drops you directly at the start of the scenic route. The drive from Tromsø to Brensholmen takes about 1 hour. Arrive early at Botnhamn to start the scenic route in morning light.

days_plan.itin_title: Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: Step into Gamle Stavanger, Norway's best-preserved old quarter: 173 white wooden houses from the 18th and 19th centuries, flower-filled facades, and cobbled lanes. Allow a good hour to explore - this is a living residential neighborhood, not a frozen museum piece.

Stavanger Cathedral (Stavanger domkirke), founded around 1125, is Norway's oldest cathedral still in active use. The original Romanesque nave contrasts with the Gothic choir rebuilt after the 1272 fire - a striking example of two-era architecture.

Øvre Holmegate, nicknamed the "street of colors" (Fargegaten), brings together brightly painted facades and a handful of trendy cafes. A striking contrast to the white houses of Gamle Stavanger, just a stone's throw away.

The Norwegian Petroleum Museum traces the oil saga from the discovery of the first North Sea fields in the 1960s. The building itself, opened in 1999, resembles an oil platform planted on the harbor - you can't miss it.
The Norwegian Canning Museum occupies a converted sardine factory: you'll see cans smoked on-site and understand how this industry built the city before oil took over. A densely packed half-hour.
The Stavanger Museum gathers collections of natural history and regional archaeology, including Viking Age artifacts. Handy for preparing tomorrow's hike with some regional context.

From the boat, you'll see Preikestolen from a completely different angle - the platform looks tiny against the 600 m of sheer wall. Many say it's even more impressive seen from below than from the summit.
Hengjanefossen waterfall plunges directly into the fjord from the cliffs: visible from the boat deck, it alone illustrates the immensity of Lysefjord.
Fantahålå cave (Vagabond's Cave) carves into the cliffs at mid-height - the cruise guide will point out the right moment to spot it from the deck.

activities.text: Stroll from Vågen harbor to the fish market (Fisketorget): fresh shrimp, smoked salmon, and the maritime atmosphere of Stavanger's waterfront. The trick here is to arrive early for the best shrimp.

The Preikestolen hike is one of Norway's most famous: 8 km round trip, 500 m elevation gain, to reach a 25x25 m platform suspended 604 m above Lysefjord. Set off before 7am - more than 300,000 hikers head here each year and crowds are fierce during the day. From Stavanger, take the ferry to Tau, then head to the Preikestolen fjellstue parking lot.

The Lysefjord cruise from Stavanger ascends the 42 km fjord between walls rising up to 1,000 m high. Allow 3 hours round trip. Book ahead in high season - boats depart in the morning from Stavanger harbor.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast
Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast
Southern Norway Road Trip: Stavanger, Preikestolen, Lysefjord & the South Coast

visits.text: Svolvær harbor is packed with rorbu—the iconic red fishermen's cabins on stilts emblematic of the Lofoten. Walk out onto the Svinøya quay to see the cod-drying racks lined up on the rocks and the statue of the Fisherman's Wife (Fiskerkona). This is the natural crossroads of the archipelago before diving south.

The Gunnar Berg Gallery at Svinøya displays the work of the painter born in Svolvær in 1863. His most famous painting depicts the Battle of Trollfjord in 1890—a revolt by small fishermen in rowboats against industrial steamships blocking the entrance to the fjord with their nets. This painting became a national political symbol and contributed to the passage of the Lofoten fishing law.

Henningsvær is a fishing village of 510 residents scattered across several islets connected by bridges, 20 minutes from Svolvær. Its football pitch is more famous than the village itself: carved into the rock since 1927, surrounded by sea on all sides and towering peaks, it's the world's most photographed amateur stadium. Climb the rock south of the pitch for the best view.

The KaviarFactory in Henningsvær is a former cod caviar production plant converted into an international contemporary art gallery. The address has become one of Norway's most unexpected art centers. Step inside even if contemporary art isn't your priority: the industrial waterfront building is worth seeing.

The white beach at Rørvikstranda, with its transparent waters, stretches along the southern coast of the Lofoten not far from Stamsund. You can swim here in July—the water reaches 15 to 17°C. The road running along the coast via Stamsund offers fine views of the cod-drying racks.

Kabelvåg Church (Vågan kirke), built in 1898, is nicknamed the Lofoten Cathedral—with 1,200 seats, it's the largest wooden church in northern Norway. It was built to accommodate the thousands of seasonal fishermen who converged on Kabelvåg in winter. Visit in the early morning: it's often open and not crowded.

The Lofoten Aquarium (Lofotakvariet) in Kabelvåg presents local marine wildlife in outdoor and indoor tanks: sea otters, seals, and Arctic fjord fish. It's a good option for families or if the weather doesn't allow hiking.

Kabelvåg is historically the first commercial center of the Lofoten, founded in the 12th century by King Øystein Magnusson who built a shelter for fishermen there—making it one of the first towns in northern Norway. Stroll through the old center around the harbor: the 19th-century wooden houses are well preserved.

activities.text: The mini-cruise into Trollfjorden is the must-do excursion from Svolvær. This arm of the sea measures only 2.5 km long by 100 meters wide at the entrance, framed by walls exceeding 1,000 meters—Trolltindan peaks at 1,084 m. Operators offer daily departures in silent electric boats or fast RIBs. Book early morning for the best light on the cliffs.

The Festvågtind hike above Henningsvær offers a plunging view over the village's islets and their bridges. The trail climbs about 5 km round-trip with 490 meters of elevation gain. Allow 3 hours for an average hiker. The summit isn't fully marked along the route—hiking boots required, avoid in wet conditions.

The Fløya hike above Svolvær is an accessible alternative to climbing Svolværgeita (the two iconic rock horns reserved for experienced climbers). Fløya is reached in 2h round-trip over 4 km and offers a view over the harbor, islets, and surrounding mountains. Start early morning to get the panorama before afternoon clouds roll in.

days_plan.itin_title: Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands
Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands
Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands

visits.text: The Ishavskatedralen (Arctic Cathedral) was inaugurated on November 19, 1965, designed by architect Jan Inge Hovig. Its 11 aluminum-clad concrete panels on each side of the roof create the triangular silhouette visible from both the bridge and the airport. Inside, don't miss Victor Sparre's stained glass mosaic (1972) on the east wall—it depicts the hand of God radiating three beams of light. Midnight sun concerts are held here in summer: check the program before your visit.

Tromsø's waterfront stretches along the Skansen district, where the old wooden harbor warehouses date back in some cases to the 1830s. This was the base from which shipowners financed Arctic expeditions in the 19th century—Tromsø was known as the Gateway to the Arctic. Walk here in the evening: the low-angle light on the colorful facades is particularly beautiful.

The Polar Museum (Polarmuseet) is housed in an 1830 customs warehouse, opened in 1978 on the exact 50th anniversary of Roald Amundsen's last departure from Tromsø. Inside you'll encounter his authentic equipment, a reconstructed Svalbard trapper's hut, and the story of Henry Rudi—a hunter who killed 713 polar bears over 27 winters and became a national hero of his era. Arrive in the morning: the museum is small and fills up quickly in the afternoons.

The Arctic-Alpine Botanical Garden is the world's northernmost botanical garden, open since 1994 on the university campus, with free admission at all hours. Its 25 collections bring together Arctic and alpine plants from every continent, from Tibet to Patagonia. The Himalayan blue poppy (Meconopsis) is its signature plant. Note that it closes under snow in winter.

The Arctic University Museum of Norway (Norges arktiske universitetsmuseum) brings together collections on Sami culture, Arctic wildlife, and High North geology. You'll find presentations on the lives of Sami reindeer herders and their seasonal migrations—a valuable counterpoint before entering the landscapes ahead. Allow 1h15 and grab a map at the entrance: signage is primarily in Norwegian.

The Skansen district contains Tromsø's best-preserved wooden houses, painted in white, yellow, and red according to local tradition. This was the old residential heart of the city, established in the late 18th century. Take a walking tour in the afternoon: the streets are quiet, the facades photogenic.

Tromsø Cathedral (Tromsø Domkirke), built in wood in 1861, is one of Norway's largest wooden cathedrals. It stands out with its understated neo-Gothic style against the waterfront architecture. If you're here on a Sunday morning, the service is open to visitors.

activities.text: The Fjellheisen cable car opened on February 22, 1961, and carries passengers in 4 minutes up to the Storsteinen plateau at 421 meters. From there, the view sweeps across the entire city on its island and the Lyngen Alps in the distance. In winter, this is one of Tromsø's best spots for northern lights. Book ahead in high season—the cabins hold only 28 people.

Head out on a boat excursion into the Kvaløysundet to observe white-tailed eagles—one of Europe's densest populations concentrates in the fjords around Tromsø. These birds, with wingspans that can exceed 2.4 meters, dive for fish at the surface. Operators offer 2-hour trips departing from the harbor; book ahead in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape
Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape
Northern Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Alta & North Cape
Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands
Arctic Norway Road Trip: Tromsø, Senja & the Lofoten Islands

places.name: Trondheim

visits.text: Nidaros Cathedral (Nidarosdomen) is the world's northernmost Gothic cathedral. Construction began in 1070 on the tomb of Saint Olav - a Viking king who died at Stiklestad in 1030 and was canonised a year later. His silver shrine was melted down for coins at the 1537 Reformation and his remains buried in a secret location within the cathedral that no one has found since.

The Archbishop's Palace (Erkebispegården), right next to the cathedral, is Scandinavia's oldest secular building still standing. It houses the Norwegian crown jewels and an archaeological exhibition on medieval Trondheim.

Bakklandet is Trondheim's most photogenic quarter - its colourful wooden houses and cobbled streets date from the 1600s. In 1965, the city council wanted to demolish the district for a four-lane highway; activists reclaimed the empty houses in 1971 and painted the façades to save them. Cross the Old Bridge (Gamle Bybro, 1681) to reach it from the centre.

The wooden storehouses on stilts (Bryggen) lining the Nidelva are Trondheim's equivalent of Bergen's Bryggen - built partly into the river to allow boats to moor directly. The view from the Old Bridge is Trondheim's postcard shot.

Kristiansten Fortress (1681), built after the great fire that devastated Trondheim the same year, dominates the town from its hilltop. From here you have the best view of the Nidelva storehouses, the cathedral and the distant fjord. Entry is free.

Ringve Museum, the national museum of music, occupies an 18th-century manor surrounded by botanical gardens. The collection of historical instruments is displayed in the original rooms - guides play live during the tour.

Munkholmen island, accessible in 10 minutes by boat from the harbour, has served variously as an execution site, Benedictine monastery (11th century), prison (17th century) and fortress. In summer, Trondheim locals swim from the beach there.

Trøndelag Folk Museum (Trøndelag Folkemuseum) at Sverresborg gathers historic farms and buildings in an open-air setting on the hills above Trondheim. The medieval Sverresborg fortress crowns the centre of the site.

Trampe, the world's only bicycle lift, has been installed since 1993 in the steep Brubakken slope in Bakklandet. You place your foot on the pedal platform, the mechanism pushes - and you ascend the steep hill effortlessly. Available in summer season.

activities.text: Wander through Bakklandet's lanes - the district concentrates independent cafés, bookshops and craft boutiques in crooked houses. Don't miss Trampe, the world's first bicycle lift, installed in the Brubakken slope in 1993.

The Ladestien coastal path follows 14 km of coastline north of Trondheim with views over Trondheimsfjord. You can walk part of it from Nedre Elvehavn - allow 2 to 3 hours for a one-way stretch to Lade and return by bus.

Musk ox safari excursion in Dovrefjell National Park, 3 hours' drive south of Trondheim. Herds of musk oxen - descendants of animals reintroduced in 1932 - graze on the plateau at 1,000 metres altitude. Local operators organise guided outings with near-guaranteed sightings in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim
Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim
Central Norway Road Trip: Ålesund, Geirangerfjord, Trollstigen & Trondheim

map_keywords: Gamle Bybro Old Town Bridge Trondheim

visits.text: Steinsdalsfossen waterfall, 2 km from Norheimsund, is one of Norway's most visited for a simple reason: you can walk behind it on foot, staying dry. A paved path follows the riverbank, climbs behind the 46-metre curtain of water and emerges cliffsside. Kaiser Wilhelm II came here every summer for 25 years - until 1914; in May-June, the snowmelt flow peaks.

The Hardanger suspension bridge (Hardangerbrua), opened in 2013, spans Hardangerfjord for 1,380 metres - one of the world's longest suspension bridges. It replaced a ferry service that connected Bruravik and Brimnes for decades. Stop at one of the panoramic viewpoints to see it in its entirety from the shore.

Ulvik, a village of a few hundred inhabitants at the head of Ulvikafjord, is nicknamed 'Norway's orchard'. The slopes around the fjord are covered with thousands of apple, pear and cherry trees - English Cistercian monks introduced fruit cultivation here in the 13th century. In May, the blossoming orchards transform the village into an Impressionist painting. Stroll along the waterfront and fjord shore.

The Ulvik Fruit and Cider Route is the only one of its kind in Norway: three family farms welcome you for cider and apple juice tastings. Hardanger cider has obtained Protected Geographical Indication - Norway's equivalent of an AOC. In autumn, harvests are at their peak.

The trails above Ulvik offer plunging views over Ulvikafjord and Osafjord - two quiet arms of Hardangerfjord encircled by orchards and birch forests. Marked local trails start directly from the village. Pack hiking shoes: some sections are steep and can be slippery in wet weather.

Vøringsfossen waterfall, 20 km from Eidfjord, is Norway's most famous: 163 metres of free fall from the Hardangervidda plateau down into Måbødalen valley. In 1880, a hotel was built right at the edge - all materials carried up by pack horse from Eidfjord. A 47-metre bridge opened in 2020, with 99 steps above the gorge, offers a vertiginous view of the drop.

activities.text: Drive Bergen-Ulvik via Norheimsund and Hardangerfjord: allow 2h30. The scenic Alvikvegen road along the fjord is worth the detour - the road hugs the water a few metres up with constant views over the green waters of Hardangerfjord and the snow-capped peaks in the background.

Scenic loop from Ulvik to Eidfjord and back via RV7 - allow half a day. The section of road that descends through the hairpin tunnels of Måbødalen valley is spectacular: the transition from lunar plateau at 1,250 m to green valley to fjord happens in a few kilometres.

days_plan.itin_title: Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
Norway Road Trip by Train: Oslo, Fjords & Bergen
